title: AWS CloudFront and Route 53 Deep Knowledge Test
description: A detailed gap-finding test for Amazon CloudFront, Route 53 DNS, and how they work together. Uses fine-grained question types: single_choice, multiple_choice, true_false, short_answer, and long_answer.

tests.title: CloudFront Core Architecture
tests.instructions: Answer from memory. For single-choice questions, pick the best answer. For multiple-choice questions, select every correct option. For written questions, explain the mechanism, not just the label.

questions.prompt: What is the primary job of Amazon CloudFront in a web architecture?
questions.expectedAnswer: To deliver content, APIs, and applications through AWS edge locations while reducing latency and origin load
options: To replace the application origin and store all application state at the edge
To deliver content, APIs, and applications through AWS edge locations while reducing latency and origin load
To provide recursive DNS resolution for VPC workloads
To register domain names and issue DNS name servers

questions.prompt: Describe the request path when a viewer requests https://www.example.com/app.js through CloudFront and the object is not already in the nearest edge cache.
rubric: Mentions DNS resolving the viewer-facing name to CloudFront
Mentions viewer connects to an edge location/POP
Explains cache lookup and miss
Mentions regional edge cache and/or origin fetch
Mentions response cached according to policies and returned to viewer

questions.prompt: In CloudFront, what is a distribution?
questions.expectedAnswer: A distribution is the CloudFront configuration that defines viewer domains, origins, cache behaviors, security settings, logging, and delivery behavior for content.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes CloudFront edge locations from Regional Edge Caches?
questions.expectedAnswer: Edge locations serve viewers directly; Regional Edge Caches sit between edge locations and origins to improve cache hit ratio
options: Edge locations serve viewers directly; Regional Edge Caches sit between edge locations and origins to improve cache hit ratio
Regional Edge Caches serve DNS answers; edge locations only store logs
Edge locations exist only in AWS Regions; Regional Edge Caches exist only inside VPCs
There is no difference; the names refer to the same cache layer

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid CloudFront origin types? Select all that apply.
rubric: Identifies S3 as a valid origin
Identifies ALB/EC2/custom HTTP/on-prem HTTP as valid origins
Identifies API Gateway and AWS media services as valid HTTP origins
Does not treat DynamoDB as a direct CloudFront origin unless fronted by an HTTP service
Gives realistic use cases
options: S3 bucket
Application Load Balancer
EC2 or custom HTTP server
API Gateway
MediaPackage or MediaStore
On-premises HTTP server
DynamoDB table directly

questions.prompt: What does a CloudFront cache behavior control?
questions.expectedAnswer: A cache behavior maps path patterns to origin and delivery rules such as allowed methods, cache policy, origin request policy, response headers policy, compression, viewer protocol policy, signed URL/cookie requirements, and edge functions.

questions.prompt: If multiple cache behaviors could match a request path, what is the safest mental model?
questions.expectedAnswer: Ordered cache behaviors are evaluated by path pattern, and the default behavior catches anything not matched
options: CloudFront randomly chooses a behavior to spread load
The default behavior always wins, even if a more specific behavior exists
Ordered cache behaviors are evaluated by path pattern, and the default behavior catches anything not matched
Route 53 chooses the behavior before the request reaches CloudFront

questions.prompt: CloudFront can only serve static objects and should not be used in front of dynamic APIs.
questions.expectedAnswer: False

questions.prompt: What problem does CloudFront Origin Shield primarily solve?
questions.expectedAnswer: It adds a centralized caching layer to reduce duplicate origin fetches across edge locations
options: It encrypts S3 objects at rest
It adds a centralized caching layer to reduce duplicate origin fetches across edge locations
It registers a domain for a CloudFront distribution
It forwards DNS queries from on-premises networks into a VPC

questions.prompt: Explain CloudFront origin failover. What signals can cause CloudFront to use the secondary origin, and what kinds of architectures benefit from it?
rubric: Explains primary and secondary origin group
Mentions configured HTTP status codes and connection/origin failures
Distinguishes CloudFront failover from Route 53 DNS failover
Gives examples such as S3 backup origin, second region, or static fallback

tests.title: CloudFront Caching, Policies, and HTTP
tests.topic: Cache keys, forwarding, TTLs, and HTTP behavior
tests.instructions: Focus on what changes the cache key, what is forwarded to origin, and what controls freshness.

questions.prompt: What is a CloudFront cache key?
questions.expectedAnswer: The cache key is the set of request attributes CloudFront uses to decide whether two viewer requests can reuse the same cached response.

questions.prompt: Which answer best describes the difference between a cache policy and an origin request policy?
questions.expectedAnswer: A cache policy controls what varies the cache key; an origin request policy controls additional viewer request data forwarded to the origin
options: A cache policy controls what varies the cache key; an origin request policy controls additional viewer request data forwarded to the origin
A cache policy controls DNS TTLs; an origin request policy controls domain registration
A cache policy only applies to S3 origins; an origin request policy only applies to ALB origins
They are two names for the exact same setting

questions.prompt: Why can forwarding all cookies, all headers, or all query strings reduce cache effectiveness?
questions.expectedAnswer: It creates many cache variants, so fewer requests share the same cached object
options: It disables HTTPS
It creates many cache variants, so fewer requests share the same cached object
It prevents CloudFront from reaching S3 origins
It forces Route 53 to lower its TTL

questions.prompt: Explain how CloudFront minimum TTL, default TTL, maximum TTL, and origin Cache-Control headers interact.
rubric: Mentions origin headers can set freshness
Mentions default TTL applies when origin does not provide cache directives
Mentions minimum TTL can keep content cached even if origin asks for shorter/no cache
Mentions maximum TTL caps origin-provided freshness

questions.prompt: What does a CloudFront invalidation do?
questions.expectedAnswer: Marks cached objects as stale/removed at CloudFront so future requests fetch fresh content
options: Deletes the original object from S3
Marks cached objects as stale/removed at CloudFront so future requests fetch fresh content
Changes the DNS name of the distribution
Rotates TLS certificates

questions.prompt: When would you prefer file versioning such as app.a1b2c3.js over CloudFront invalidations, and when would invalidation still be useful?
rubric: Explains versioned filenames avoid stale-cache ambiguity
Mentions long TTLs for immutable assets
Mentions invalidation for HTML, emergency corrections, or unversioned paths
Considers cost/operational simplicity

questions.prompt: Which cache behavior setting matters when placing CloudFront in front of an API that uses POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE?
questions.expectedAnswer: Allowed HTTP methods
options: Allowed HTTP methods
Price class only
DNSSEC signing
Registrar contact privacy

questions.prompt: CloudFront can cache responses to every HTTP method, including POST and DELETE, in the same way it caches GET.
questions.expectedAnswer: False

questions.prompt: What does CloudFront automatic compression do, and what request header is usually involved?
questions.expectedAnswer: CloudFront can compress eligible objects before returning them to viewers, commonly based on the viewer's Accept-Encoding header such as gzip or Brotli support.

questions.prompt: Which CloudFront feature is best suited for adding headers like HSTS, CSP, CORS, or custom security headers without changing origin code?
questions.expectedAnswer: Response headers policy
options: Response headers policy
Origin Shield
Weighted DNS routing
Route 53 Resolver rule

tests.title: CloudFront Security, Access, and TLS
tests.topic: HTTPS, origin protection, WAF, signed access, and privacy
tests.instructions: Think through both viewer-to-CloudFront and CloudFront-to-origin security.

questions.prompt: Explain the two separate TLS connections in a typical CloudFront HTTPS request: viewer to CloudFront and CloudFront to origin. What can be configured independently?
rubric: Identifies viewer connection and origin connection as separate
Mentions viewer certificate/custom domain on CloudFront
Mentions origin protocol policy and origin TLS validation
Mentions redirect HTTP to HTTPS or HTTPS-only viewer policies

questions.prompt: For a custom domain on a CloudFront distribution, where must the ACM certificate generally be created?
questions.expectedAnswer: In us-east-1 for CloudFront viewer certificates
options: In the same Region as the S3 bucket
In us-east-1 for CloudFront viewer certificates
In every edge location manually
In Route 53, not ACM

questions.prompt: What is the modern purpose of CloudFront Origin Access Control (OAC) for S3 origins?
questions.expectedAnswer: To restrict S3 bucket access so CloudFront can fetch private objects while direct public access stays blocked
options: To let viewers bypass CloudFront and read directly from S3
To restrict S3 bucket access so CloudFront can fetch private objects while direct public access stays blocked
To create DNS records automatically for every S3 object
To encrypt query strings before they reach the browser

questions.prompt: What is the relationship between Origin Access Identity (OAI) and Origin Access Control (OAC)?
questions.expectedAnswer: Both restrict S3 origins to CloudFront access, but OAC is the newer recommended mechanism with broader support and SigV4-based requests.

questions.prompt: When would signed cookies usually be a better fit than signed URLs?
questions.expectedAnswer: When a user needs temporary access to many private objects under a path
options: When a user needs temporary access to many private objects under a path
When only DNS health checks are needed
When the origin is public and no authorization is desired
When registering a domain

questions.prompt: What does CloudFront geographic restriction do?
questions.expectedAnswer: Allows or blocks viewer access based on viewer country
options: Routes DNS queries to the closest AWS Region
Allows or blocks viewer access based on viewer country
Moves the S3 bucket into the user's country
Encrypts content differently in each country

questions.prompt: Explain how CloudFront, AWS WAF, AWS Shield, and Route 53 can work together as an edge security layer.
rubric: Mentions CloudFront as edge front door
Mentions WAF rules for application-layer filtering
Mentions Shield for DDoS protection
Mentions Route 53 DNS and health/failover where relevant
Explains reducing exposure of origins

questions.prompt: What is CloudFront field-level encryption intended to protect?
questions.expectedAnswer: Selected sensitive form fields or data fields so only designated private keys can decrypt them downstream
options: Selected sensitive form fields or data fields so only designated private keys can decrypt them downstream
The entire S3 bucket from all AWS principals
DNS zone transfers between name servers
CloudWatch logs at rest only

questions.prompt: For a custom origin behind CloudFront, why might you add a secret custom header from CloudFront to the origin and validate it at the origin?
questions.expectedAnswer: It helps the origin reject requests that do not come through CloudFront, reducing direct-origin bypass risk.

questions.prompt: Which CloudFront-related controls can restrict access or reduce abuse? Select all that apply.
rubric: Correctly classifies signed URLs/cookies as viewer authorization
Correctly classifies geo restriction as country-based viewer control
Correctly classifies OAC/OAI as origin access protection
Correctly classifies WAF/Shield as attack mitigation
Correctly classifies response headers policy as browser/client security hardening
Does not claim private hosted zones directly secure a public CloudFront distribution
options: Signed URLs/cookies
Geo restriction
OAC/OAI
AWS WAF
AWS Shield
Response headers policy
Route 53 private hosted zone

tests.title: CloudFront Edge Compute, DevOps, and Observability
tests.topic: CloudFront Functions, Lambda@Edge, KVS, deployments, logs, metrics, and cost
tests.instructions: Pay attention to the differences between similar-looking features.

questions.prompt: Which statement best compares CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge?
questions.expectedAnswer: CloudFront Functions are for lightweight, very low-latency viewer request/response logic; Lambda@Edge supports heavier Node.js/Python logic and origin events
options: CloudFront Functions are for lightweight, very low-latency viewer request/response logic; Lambda@Edge supports heavier Node.js/Python logic and origin events
Lambda@Edge is only for DNS records; CloudFront Functions are only for S3 bucket policies
They are identical runtimes with identical limits
CloudFront Functions run inside VPC subnets; Lambda@Edge runs only in Route 53

questions.prompt: Give four good CloudFront Functions use cases and two use cases that should probably move to Lambda@Edge or the origin instead.
rubric: Good examples include redirects, header manipulation, URL rewrites, cache-key normalization, simple auth/token checks
Moves network calls, heavy computation, large dependencies, or origin response processing away from CloudFront Functions
Explains latency and runtime constraints

questions.prompt: What is CloudFront KeyValueStore used for?
questions.expectedAnswer: Storing lookup data for CloudFront Functions without redeploying function code for every data change
options: Storing lookup data for CloudFront Functions without redeploying function code for every data change
Replacing DynamoDB for strongly consistent transactional workloads
Storing Route 53 hosted zone records
Keeping CloudFront standard logs before delivery to S3

questions.prompt: Name the CloudFront event phases commonly associated with Lambda@Edge.
questions.expectedAnswer: Viewer request, origin request, origin response, and viewer response.

questions.prompt: What does CloudFront continuous deployment let you do?
questions.expectedAnswer: Gradually test a staging distribution configuration against production traffic without changing DNS
options: Gradually test a staging distribution configuration against production traffic without changing DNS
Automatically register new domains in Route 53
Run SQL migrations at edge locations
Bypass all cache behaviors for blue/green deploys

questions.prompt: Which answer best compares CloudFront standard logs and real-time logs?
questions.expectedAnswer: Standard logs are delivered to S3 after a delay; real-time logs are delivered to Kinesis Data Streams within seconds with configurable sampling
options: Standard logs are delivered to S3 after a delay; real-time logs are delivered to Kinesis Data Streams within seconds with configurable sampling
Standard logs are only for Route 53; real-time logs are only for S3 origins
Real-time logs are less detailed and cannot be filtered
Both are delivered only to CloudWatch Logs

questions.prompt: Name at least four CloudFront metrics or observability signals you would check during a performance or availability investigation.
rubric: Includes requests, bytes downloaded/uploaded, 4xx/5xx error rates, cache hit rate, origin latency, total error rate, real-time logs, or CloudWatch metrics

questions.prompt: What is a CloudFront price class?
questions.expectedAnswer: A setting that limits which groups of edge locations CloudFront uses to balance cost and global performance
options: A setting that limits which groups of edge locations CloudFront uses to balance cost and global performance
A Route 53 pricing tier for domain names
A certificate validation class
A cache invalidation priority level

questions.prompt: Which pricing option trades a committed monthly CloudFront spend for a discount and can include AWS WAF usage benefits?
questions.expectedAnswer: CloudFront Security Savings Bundle
options: CloudFront Security Savings Bundle
Route 53 Resolver endpoint bundle
S3 Intelligent-Tiering
ACM public certificate surcharge

questions.prompt: A static JS file behind CloudFront is missing cache far more often than expected. List the likely causes you would investigate and what evidence you would look for.
rubric: Checks cache policy headers/cookies/query strings
Checks origin Cache-Control and TTL settings
Checks object versioning and invalidations
Checks path behavior match
Uses CloudFront headers/logs/metrics to verify cache result

tests.title: Route 53 DNS Foundations
tests.topic: Authoritative DNS, hosted zones, records, aliases, TTLs, and domain registration
tests.instructions: Explain DNS mechanics precisely. Avoid hand-wavy answers like 'Route 53 points things somewhere'.

questions.prompt: What does it mean that Route 53 is an authoritative DNS service?
questions.expectedAnswer: It answers DNS queries for zones you host in Route 53 using records you configure
options: It answers DNS queries for zones you host in Route 53 using records you configure
It recursively resolves every DNS query from every browser on the internet
It replaces HTTP routing inside CloudFront
It stores SSL private keys for domains

questions.prompt: What is a Route 53 hosted zone?
questions.expectedAnswer: A hosted zone is a container for DNS records for a domain or subdomain, either public for internet DNS or private for VPC DNS.

questions.prompt: Describe how a domain registered outside AWS can delegate DNS hosting to a public hosted zone in Route 53.
rubric: Create public hosted zone for the domain
Copy the Route 53 authoritative name servers
Update name servers at the current registrar
Understand registrar delegation vs DNS records
Mentions propagation/caching delay

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes public vs private hosted zones?
questions.expectedAnswer: Public hosted zones answer internet DNS queries; private hosted zones answer DNS queries from associated VPCs
options: Public hosted zones answer internet DNS queries; private hosted zones answer DNS queries from associated VPCs
Private hosted zones are encrypted public zones
Public hosted zones can only contain A records; private zones can only contain CNAME records
Private hosted zones are used only for domain registration

questions.prompt: Briefly explain these DNS record types and give one use case for each: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS, SOA, CAA, SRV.
rubric: Defines address records A/AAAA
Defines CNAME aliasing limits
Defines email-related MX and TXT examples
Defines delegation/zone metadata NS and SOA
Defines CAA certificate authority authorization
Defines SRV service discovery

questions.prompt: What is a Route 53 alias record?
questions.expectedAnswer: A Route 53-specific record that maps a DNS name to certain AWS resources such as CloudFront, ELB, S3 website endpoints, or another record, often usable at the zone apex
options: A Route 53-specific record that maps a DNS name to certain AWS resources such as CloudFront, ELB, S3 website endpoints, or another record, often usable at the zone apex
A public/private key pair for DNSSEC
A recursive Resolver forwarding rule
A mandatory replacement for every TXT record

questions.prompt: Why is an alias record commonly used for example.com pointing at CloudFront instead of a normal CNAME?
questions.expectedAnswer: DNS standards do not allow a CNAME at the zone apex when other required records such as NS/SOA exist; Route 53 alias solves this for supported targets
options: DNS standards do not allow a CNAME at the zone apex when other required records such as NS/SOA exist; Route 53 alias solves this for supported targets
CloudFront refuses all CNAMEs
Alias records are the only records that support HTTPS
CNAME records only work inside private hosted zones

questions.prompt: What does TTL mean in DNS, and why does it matter during a migration?
questions.expectedAnswer: TTL is how long resolvers can cache a DNS answer. Lowering it before a migration can reduce how long old answers persist, but existing cached answers keep their original TTL.

questions.prompt: Registering a domain with Route 53 and hosting DNS for that domain in Route 53 are related but separate capabilities.
questions.expectedAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Explain DNSSEC signing for a public hosted zone and DNSSEC validation in Resolver. What threat does DNSSEC reduce, and what operational mistake can break resolution?
rubric: Explains cryptographic signing/validation of DNS data
Mentions protection against spoofed/tampered DNS responses
Distinguishes hosted zone signing from resolver validation
Mentions DS/key chain mistakes can cause validation failure

tests.title: Route 53 Routing, Health Checks, and Traffic Management
tests.topic: Routing policies, health checks, failover, and Traffic Flow
tests.instructions: For each policy, know what input Route 53 uses to choose an answer and what problem it solves.

questions.prompt: When is simple routing the best fit?
questions.expectedAnswer: One record name should return one or more fixed values without policy-based traffic steering
options: One record name should return one or more fixed values without policy-based traffic steering
You need different answers based on viewer geography
You need active/passive failover
You need CIDR-based answers by client network

questions.prompt: Explain weighted routing. Give two use cases and one caveat caused by DNS caching.
rubric: Explains weights influence answer distribution
Use cases include canary, blue/green, A/B, gradual migration
Mentions caching means exact per-request percentages are not guaranteed
Mentions health checks can remove unhealthy weighted records

questions.prompt: What does latency-based routing optimize for?
questions.expectedAnswer: Routing users to the AWS Region that Route 53 estimates has the lowest latency from their resolver/location
options: Routing users to the AWS Region that Route 53 estimates has the lowest latency from their resolver/location
Routing to the cheapest AWS Region
Routing based only on the domain registrar's country
Routing to the origin with the newest TLS certificate

questions.prompt: In Route 53 failover routing, what is the usual role of health checks?
questions.expectedAnswer: They determine when Route 53 should stop returning the primary answer and return the secondary answer
options: They determine when Route 53 should stop returning the primary answer and return the secondary answer
They compress HTTP responses
They decide which CloudFront cache behavior matches a path
They create ACM certificates

questions.prompt: Compare geolocation routing and geoproximity routing. When would you use each?
rubric: Geolocation routes by user geographic location such as country/continent/subdivision
Geoproximity routes by geographic distance between users and resources
Mentions bias/shifting traffic for geoproximity where relevant
Gives data residency/content localization vs nearest-resource examples

questions.prompt: What does Route 53 IP-based routing use to select an answer?
questions.expectedAnswer: CIDR blocks associated with the query-originating client/resolver network
options: CIDR blocks associated with the query-originating client/resolver network
The HTTP X-Forwarded-For header
CloudFront cache keys
The user's browser language header

questions.prompt: What is multivalue answer routing, and how is it different from an Elastic Load Balancer?
questions.expectedAnswer: Multivalue answer routing can return multiple healthy DNS records, but it is DNS-level answer selection, not a full request-level load balancer with connection management.

questions.prompt: List and explain the main kinds of Route 53 health checks you might use for DNS failover.
rubric: Endpoint checks such as HTTP/HTTPS/TCP
Calculated health checks combining child checks
CloudWatch alarm based checks
String matching where relevant
Mentions health checkers and regional/global behavior at a high level

questions.prompt: What is Route 53 Traffic Flow for?
questions.expectedAnswer: Building and managing visual/global traffic policies that combine routing methods such as latency, geoproximity, health, and weights
options: Building and managing visual/global traffic policies that combine routing methods such as latency, geoproximity, health, and weights
Streaming CloudFront real-time logs
Encrypting DNS answers with TLS
Creating Lambda@Edge functions

questions.prompt: Choose the best Route 53 routing policy for each scenario and justify it: gradual migration, active/passive DR, user-to-nearest-region, country-specific content, enterprise-customer CIDR routing, multiple healthy IP answers.
rubric: Weighted for gradual migration
Failover for active/passive DR
Latency or geoproximity for nearest-region depending on intent
Geolocation for country-specific content
IP-based routing for customer CIDRs
Multivalue answer for multiple healthy IPs

tests.title: Route 53 Resolver, Private DNS, DNS Firewall, and Profiles
tests.topic: Hybrid DNS, VPC resolution, DNS security, and multi-account configuration
tests.instructions: Think in terms of recursive resolution paths inside VPCs and between AWS and on-premises networks.

questions.prompt: What is Route 53 Resolver primarily used for?
questions.expectedAnswer: Recursive DNS resolution for VPCs and hybrid DNS forwarding between AWS and other networks
options: Recursive DNS resolution for VPCs and hybrid DNS forwarding between AWS and other networks
Serving public authoritative DNS for your internet domain only
Caching CloudFront objects
Issuing TLS certificates

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoints, outbound endpoints, and Resolver rules.
rubric: Inbound endpoints let external/on-prem DNS query into VPC Resolver
Outbound endpoints let VPC DNS forward queries to external/on-prem DNS
Rules define which domains are forwarded and to which target DNS servers
Mentions security groups/subnets/IPs at a high level

questions.prompt: How does a private hosted zone become resolvable from a VPC?
questions.expectedAnswer: By associating the private hosted zone with the VPC
options: By associating the private hosted zone with the VPC
By creating a public NS record at the registrar
By enabling CloudFront Origin Shield
By adding a CNAME at the zone apex

questions.prompt: What is split-horizon DNS, and how can Route 53 support it?
questions.expectedAnswer: Split-horizon DNS returns different answers for the same name depending on where the query comes from, commonly using a public hosted zone for internet clients and a private hosted zone for VPC clients.

questions.prompt: What does Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall do?
questions.expectedAnswer: Filters outbound DNS queries from VPCs using domain lists and rule groups
options: Filters outbound DNS queries from VPCs using domain lists and rule groups
Blocks HTTP requests at CloudFront based on URI path
Encrypts S3 objects
Registers malicious domains so nobody else can use them

questions.prompt: Describe practical DNS Firewall actions or outcomes you would expect when a VPC workload queries a blocked domain. Include what you would log or monitor.
rubric: Mentions block/allow/alert-style policy behavior
Mentions returning NODATA/NXDOMAIN/override or redirection where configured
Mentions query logging and CloudWatch/S3/Kinesis destinations where relevant
Connects to malware/data-exfiltration control

questions.prompt: What does Route 53 Resolver query logging help you see?
questions.expectedAnswer: DNS queries made by resources in VPCs, useful for troubleshooting and security analysis
options: DNS queries made by resources in VPCs, useful for troubleshooting and security analysis
CloudFront cache hit ratio for public viewers
Every HTTP header sent to an ALB
All domain registration invoices

questions.prompt: What problem do Route 53 Profiles help solve?
questions.expectedAnswer: Managing and sharing Route 53 configurations such as private hosted zones, Resolver rules, and DNS Firewall rule groups across VPCs/accounts
options: Managing and sharing Route 53 configurations such as private hosted zones, Resolver rules, and DNS Firewall rule groups across VPCs/accounts
Choosing CloudFront edge price classes
Creating HTML profiles for users
Replacing IAM roles for all DNS changes

questions.prompt: What is Route 53 Resolver on Outposts endpoints intended for?
questions.expectedAnswer: DNS resolution connectivity between Outposts racks and on-premises or other network DNS environments
options: DNS resolution connectivity between Outposts racks and on-premises or other network DNS environments
Serving CloudFront content from an Outposts rack
Registering domains that end in .outposts
Creating browser cache headers

questions.prompt: Design DNS for a company with VPCs in AWS, an on-premises data center, private service names under corp.example.com, and public names under example.com. Explain hosted zones, Resolver endpoints, forwarding rules, and likely failure points.
rubric: Uses public hosted zone for internet-facing example.com records
Uses private hosted zone or on-prem authoritative DNS for corp.example.com
Uses inbound/outbound endpoints and forwarding rules appropriately
Mentions security groups, network paths, endpoint IP availability, and rule association
Mentions split-horizon risks and testing with dig/nslookup

tests.title: CloudFront and Route 53 Integration
tests.topic: Custom domains, aliases, TLS, failover boundaries, and full request lifecycle
tests.instructions: These questions test where DNS stops and HTTP/CDN behavior begins.

questions.prompt: List the main steps to serve https://www.example.com from CloudFront using Route 53 for DNS.
rubric: Request/validate ACM certificate for www.example.com in us-east-1
Add alternate domain name/CNAME to CloudFront distribution
Configure origin and cache behaviors
Create Route 53 alias A/AAAA record for www.example.com to CloudFront
Set viewer protocol policy/security settings and test

questions.prompt: Which Route 53 record is typically used to point example.com, the zone apex, to a CloudFront distribution?
questions.expectedAnswer: Alias A and optionally alias AAAA
options: Alias A and optionally alias AAAA
MX
SOA
SRV only

questions.prompt: Which setting is controlled by CloudFront rather than Route 53?
questions.expectedAnswer: Cache policy for forwarding query strings to the origin
options: Cache policy for forwarding query strings to the origin
Hosted zone name server delegation
Domain registration contact details
DNS TTL on a non-alias record

questions.prompt: Route 53 can route /api/* to one target and /images/* to another target because DNS sees the full URL path.
questions.expectedAnswer: False

questions.prompt: If DNS cannot see URL paths, where should /api/* versus /images/* routing usually be implemented?
questions.expectedAnswer: In CloudFront cache behaviors, an application load balancer, API gateway, reverse proxy, or application routing layer.

questions.prompt: Compare Route 53 DNS failover and CloudFront origin failover. When would you use one, the other, or both?
rubric: Route 53 failover changes DNS answers based on health checks
CloudFront origin failover changes origin selection behind one distribution
DNS failover affected by TTL/resolver caching
CloudFront failover can be faster for origin-level HTTP errors
Both can be layered for distribution-level or regional disaster recovery

questions.prompt: Why is latency-based Route 53 routing usually not needed just to send global users to their nearest CloudFront edge?
questions.expectedAnswer: CloudFront's own anycast/edge network already routes viewers to nearby edge infrastructure after DNS resolves the distribution name
options: CloudFront's own anycast/edge network already routes viewers to nearby edge infrastructure after DNS resolves the distribution name
CloudFront only works in one Region
Route 53 latency routing is illegal with CloudFront
Latency routing disables HTTPS

questions.prompt: When might you intentionally use Route 53 routing policies across multiple CloudFront distributions instead of one global distribution?
rubric: Examples include regional isolation, separate compliance boundaries, different distributions/configs, DR, tenant separation, migration/canary
Acknowledges added complexity with certificates, aliases, caching, logs, invalidations
Does not claim this is required for normal global edge performance

questions.prompt: Why might you create both alias A and alias AAAA records to CloudFront?
questions.expectedAnswer: To support both IPv4 and IPv6 viewers when IPv6 is enabled on the distribution
options: To support both IPv4 and IPv6 viewers when IPv6 is enabled on the distribution
To double the cache hit ratio
To make HTTP POST cacheable
To enable private hosted zones

questions.prompt: Walk through the complete lifecycle for a first-time visitor opening https://example.com/: registrar delegation, Route 53 hosted zone, alias record, CloudFront TLS, cache behavior, origin fetch, response, and subsequent cached request.
rubric: Covers delegation from registrar to Route 53 name servers
Covers Route 53 alias answer to CloudFront
Covers TLS/SNI/custom certificate
Covers behavior selection and cache lookup
Covers origin fetch and caching
Covers subsequent request served from cache when fresh

tests.title: Scenario Gap Finder
tests.instructions: These are harder. Write your reasoning, assumptions, and tradeoffs. The goal is to expose gaps.

questions.prompt: Users report stale HTML after a deploy, but JS/CSS assets are fine. Design a CloudFront caching strategy for HTML and static assets, including TTLs, Cache-Control, invalidation, and file versioning.
rubric: Short/no-cache or carefully controlled TTL for HTML
Long immutable TTL for versioned assets
Invalidation for emergency or unversioned HTML paths
Explains browser cache vs CloudFront cache
Avoids forwarding unnecessary cookies/headers for static assets

questions.prompt: You need to serve a private S3-backed website through CloudFront only, with no public S3 access. Describe the setup and the policy pieces.
rubric: Uses CloudFront distribution with S3 origin
Uses OAC as preferred approach
S3 Block Public Access remains enabled where appropriate
Bucket policy allows CloudFront service principal/source distribution
Route 53 alias and ACM certificate for custom domain

questions.prompt: Design CloudFront in front of an API. What would you configure differently from a static asset distribution?
rubric: Allowed methods include API verbs
Cache disabled or carefully scoped for personalized/dynamic responses
Forward required headers/query/cookies only
Origin request policy and CORS/security headers
WAF/rate limiting/auth considerations
Logs/metrics for errors and latency

questions.prompt: Design active/passive disaster recovery for a public app using Route 53, CloudFront, and two origins in different Regions. Explain failover points and TTL/caching consequences.
rubric: Uses Route 53 failover where DNS-level target should change
Uses CloudFront origin failover where one distribution can switch origins
Mentions health checks and what they check
Mentions DNS TTL and resolver caching
Mentions cache behavior when origin changes
Mentions data consistency/session implications

questions.prompt: An EC2 instance cannot resolve db.corp.example.com, which is hosted on-premises. You use Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoints. Give a step-by-step troubleshooting path.
rubric: Check VPC DNS settings and DHCP options if relevant
Check Resolver rule domain match and VPC association
Check outbound endpoint status, subnets, IPs, and security groups
Check network path to on-prem DNS through VPN/DX/firewall
Check on-prem DNS recursion/authoritative response
Use dig/nslookup and query logs

questions.prompt: Plan a low-risk migration of public DNS for example.com from another provider to Route 53. Include inventory, TTL strategy, validation, delegation, rollback, and post-cutover checks.
rubric: Exports/inventories existing records
Creates hosted zone and imports records
Lowers TTLs ahead of time where possible
Validates answers against Route 53 name servers before delegation
Updates registrar name servers
Monitors propagation and keeps old zone temporarily
Has rollback by restoring old delegation if needed

questions.prompt: Security review: A public ALB origin is behind CloudFront, but attackers can call the ALB DNS name directly. What controls would you consider, and what are their tradeoffs?
rubric: Restrict ALB security group to CloudFront managed prefix list where applicable
Validate secret custom header or use origin auth where possible
Use WAF at CloudFront and/or ALB with clear tradeoffs
Consider private origins/VPC architecture where applicable
Discuss operational rotation and bypass limitations

questions.prompt: A CloudFront bill is high and origin load is still high. What would you inspect before changing architecture?
rubric: Cache hit ratio and cache key cardinality
Forwarded headers/cookies/query strings
TTL and Cache-Control
Origin Shield for duplicate origin fetch collapse
Invalidation patterns and object versioning
Price class, data transfer, real-time log sampling, WAF costs
Separate static and dynamic behaviors

questions.prompt: Design a Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rollout for multiple AWS accounts. Include rule groups, domain lists, alert/block modes, exception handling, logging, and how Profiles might help.
rubric: Central rule groups/domain lists
Start in alert/count style before blocking critical domains
Exception process for business domains
Query logging and monitoring
Multi-account/VPC association strategy
Profiles or AWS RAM style sharing where relevant

questions.prompt: Teach back the difference between CloudFront and Route 53 to a junior engineer in under 10 sentences. Include what each service does, where they overlap, where they do not, and one example of using both together.
rubric: CloudFront is CDN/edge HTTP delivery and caching/security front door
Route 53 is DNS/domain registration/authoritative and resolver service
Overlap at edge availability/security but not same layer
DNS maps names; CloudFront handles HTTP/TLS/cache/origin behavior
Example uses Route 53 alias to CloudFront with custom domain
